meta.title: W168 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes A-Class W168 engine — M166 petrol and OM668 diesel. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the A210 Evolution explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes A-Class W168 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned W168 engine typically costs £1,200–£4,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume M166 E14 petrol sits at £1,200–£2,200, while the M166 E21 A210 Evolution is the most expensive at £2,200–£4,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,000 depending on specialist rates. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes A-Class W168 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M166 E19 (A190, 1.9-litre petrol) is widely considered the pick of the range — offering 125 PS with fewer reported issues than the smaller 1.4-litre and 1.6-litre M166 variants. The OM668 diesel line is generally robust but injector and EGR wear is common on higher-mileage examples.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes A-Class W168?

acceptedAnswer.text: Ignition coil failure on the M166 petrol engines, injector wear and EGR clogging on the OM668 diesels, and engine mount wear unique to the W168's sandwich-concept design. Cooling system leaks are also reported on higher-mileage A190 and A210 Evolution models.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes A-Class W168 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Reliability is average for its era — the M166 petrol fours are simple and durable when maintained, while the OM668 diesel is robust but sensitive to injector health and short journeys. The W168's biggest reliability challenge is its age (most examples are now 20+ years old) rather than any fundamental design flaw.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes A-Class W168 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM668 diesel without documented injector service history is the one purchase we advise caution on — injector wear can lead to bore wash and turbo damage, a repair that frequently exceeds the car's value. The A210 Evolution is rare and desirable but parts are harder to find and more expensive.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my W168 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Persistent misfires or rough idle on petrol engines (coil pack failure), poor cold starting or excessive smoke on diesels (injector/EGR issues), and loud clunking or vibration from the engine bay (worn mounts) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes A-Class W168 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £4,000+ W168, a £1,800 M166 recon is 45% of the car's value — a serious but viable repair. Against a tired £1,500 example, the same recon is 120% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line, but note that W168 values have started to rise for low-mileage, well-maintained examples.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes A-Class W168 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M166 petrol fours can comfortably exceed 150,000–200,000 miles with regular servicing and prompt coil pack replacement. The OM668 diesel can reach similar mileages but requires injector attention around 80,000–130,000 miles. The sandwich concept's engine mounts typically need replacement every 70,000–120,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the W168 and W169 A-Class?

acceptedAnswer.text: The W168 (1997–2004) is the first-generation A-Class — the one that failed the elk test and introduced the sandwich concept. The W169 (2004–2012) is the second generation, with revised styling, larger dimensions, and improved dynamics. Engines are generally not interchangeable between the two generations. See our W169 page for that generation's specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes A-Class W168 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (under the bonnet or on the driver's door pillar) and on the engine block itself — the M166 petrol engines are visible from above, while the OM668 diesel requires slight disassembly to access the block stamping. Tell us your variant (A140, A160, A190, A210, A160 CDI, A170 CDI) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes A-Class W168 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation A-Class — from the 1.4-litre M166 petrol to the 2.1-litre A210 Evolution and the OM668 turbodiesel that defined Mercedes' first front-wheel-drive car. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for this pioneering supermini. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes A-Class W168 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the W168 hatchback specifically — including the full M166 petrol range and the OM668 turbodiesel line.

overview.intro: The W168 is the first-generation Mercedes-Benz A-Class, launched in 1997 as the brand's entry-level model and its first front-wheel-drive passenger car. It introduced the "sandwich concept" — a raised floorpan that allowed the engine to slide underneath in a collision. The engine range spanned M166 four-cylinder petrol units from 1.4 to 2.1 litres, plus OM668 1.7-litre turbodiesels, with a facelift in 2001 bringing updated power outputs.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W168 (first generation) • Predecessor: None (first A-Class) • Successor: W169 (2004) • Sibling body styles in this generation: Five-door hatchback only (Vaneo MPV shared platform).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The W168 A-Class generated UK engine enquiries in 2025 [PATTERN DATA], with the OM668 diesel and M166 petrol fours remaining the most commonly discussed among specialists.

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode — one paragraph: The W168's most dependable engine is the M166 E19 (A190) — the 1.9-litre petrol four offers 125 PS and is widely considered the pick of the M166 range for its balance of power and reliability. The M166 E21 (A210 Evolution) is the most powerful at 140 PS, but its rarity makes it a niche choice. The OM668 diesel line is generally robust but suffers from typical common-rail injector and EGR issues as mileage climbs. For most buyers, the A160 petrol (1.6L, 102 PS) represents the best value — cheap to run, simple to maintain, and free of the diesel's DPF-related complications. Avoid any W168 without documented service history, as the sandwich-concept engine layout makes labour more expensive than on conventional hatches.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Budget Choice — cheapest W168 to run
Best Value — the sensible petrol choice
Most Reliable — the pick of the range
Rare Find — collectable but costly to maintain
Best Diesel — more power, same reliability

economicsRule.title: The W168 rule
economicsRule.text: A £1,800–£3,200 recon on a sub-£2,000 W168 sits at 90–160% of the car's value — at that point, scrap value usually wins. Against a well-kept £4,000+ example, the same recon drops to 45–80% — still a serious decision, but one that can make sense if the car is otherwise sound. Value the car first, then the engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W168 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: A140 (M166 E14) — cheapest parts and simplest engine.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W168 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: A210 Evolution (M166 E21) — rare parts and cooling system complexity.

keyTakeaways.question: Which W168 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: A160 (M166 E16) — enough power, simple mechanics, widely available.

problems.title: M166 Petrol Ignition Coil Failure
problems.affectedModels: W168 A140, A160, A190 (all M166 petrol variants).
problems.rootCause: The M166's coil-on-plug ignition system is prone to coil pack failure, leading to misfires, rough idle, and reduced power. The issue is exacerbated by age and heat cycling in the tightly packed engine bay.

tieredCosts.work: Replace faulty coil pack(s) and spark plugs
Full engine strip and rebuild if misfire has caused bore damage

problems.recommendation: At the first sign of a misfire or rough idle, have the coil packs and spark plugs replaced — £150–£300 is cheap insurance against the £1,200+ replacement that follows if a persistent misfire damages the cylinder bore.

cta.label: Compare M166 engine prices for your W168 A-Class →

problems.title: OM668 Diesel Injector Wear & EGR Clogging
problems.affectedModels: W168 A160 CDI, A170 CDI (all OM668 diesel variants).
problems.rootCause: The OM668's common-rail injectors wear over time, causing poor cold starting, increased smoke, and reduced fuel economy. The EGR valve clogs with soot, particularly on cars used for short urban journeys.

tieredCosts.work: Full replacement of injectors, EGR, and DPF if damaged

problems.recommendation: Use premium diesel and give the car a regular motorway run to keep the EGR and DPF clear — a £300 injector service every 80,000 miles is far cheaper than the £1,800+ replacement that follows if injector wear leads to bore wash or turbo damage.

cta.label: Compare OM668 engine prices for your W168 A-Class →

problems.title: Sandwich Concept Engine Mount Wear
problems.rootCause: The W168's unique "sandwich" design mounts the engine at a 59-degree angle just behind the front axle. The engine mounts and subframe bushes wear over time, causing clunking, vibration, and misalignment that can accelerate drivetrain wear.

tieredCosts.work: Replace engine mounts and subframe bushes
Full subframe removal and reinforcement if mounts have failed completely

problems.recommendation: Have the engine mounts inspected at every service after 70,000 miles — £300–£500 in preventative work is a fraction of the £900+ cost if worn mounts cause subframe damage or drivetrain misalignment.

cta.label: Compare W168 engine mount replacement quotes →
